title: Shark and bake
subtitle: Trinidadian fried fish in fried bread
excerpt: Seasoned fish fillets fried in hot oil and stuffed into a pillow of soft, freshly fried bread, then dressed with pepper sauce, chadon beni (coriander) sauce, mango chutney, and pineapple. Shark and bake is the signature street food of Maracas Beach in Trinidad — the dish that Trinidadians and visitors alike eat at the beach while still in their swim clothes. The bread (bake) is as important as the fish.
sourceNotes: Shark and bake originated at Maracas Beach in northwest Trinidad, where vendors have been serving shark in fried bread since at least the 1970s. Shark (usually blacktip reef shark) is the traditional fish, though kingfish and other firm white fish are also used. In the UK, cod or hake is the nearest available substitute. The dish is not widely replicable outside Trinidad because of the specific beach-vendor chutneys, but a home version captures the essential character.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The bake dough can be made 1 hour ahead. Fry just before serving.

content.text: The bake is a round, fried bread — similar in concept to Johnny cake but made to be split and filled. It must be fried fresh and split open immediately while hot to receive the fish. The hot steam inside the bread as it is opened is part of the eating experience. Fry the bake just before you are ready to eat.

Shark is unavailable in Britain. Cod fillet is the closest available substitute in terms of texture — firm enough to hold its shape when fried and to eat out of hand. Hake or pollock also work. The traditional shark seasoning (green seasoning, garlic, lime) works with any firm white fish.
Pat the {{cod-fillet}} dry. Rub with {{green-seasoning}}, {{garlic}}, lime juice, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Marinate for 15 minutes.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} (300 g), {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} (0.25 tsp), and {{caster-sugar}}. Add enough warm water (about 140 ml) to make a soft, smooth dough. Knead briefly for 2 minutes. Divide into 4 balls. Press each ball into a round about 12 cm across and 1.5 cm thick.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 170°C. Fry the bake rounds for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden and puffed. Drain and keep warm. Dust the marinated fish in {{plain-flour}} (50 g) and fry for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through. Split each bake while hot and insert a fish fillet. Dress with pepper sauce, coriander chutney, and mango chutney.

items.cause: Dough too stiff or oil not hot enough
items.fix: The dough should be softer than bread dough. Test the oil temperature — the bake should puff as soon as it hits the oil
Cod fillets should be at least 2 cm thick for frying. Cook only until golden — overcooked fish flakes apart and is impossible to eat in a bread roll
Insert a knife and check the centre is cooked through. A properly cooked bake splits cleanly at the equator with steam escaping

content.text: Shark and bake at Maracas Beach is one of the defining food experiences of Trinidad. The vendors at Maracas have been serving it since the 1970s and the queue at Richard's Shark and Bake is a fixture of any visit to the beach. The specific character of the dish comes from the multiple chutneys (the pepper sauce, the chadon beni sauce, the mango chutney, the shadow beni sauce) that each vendor makes to their own recipe. Without those chutneys the home version is only part of the experience, but the core of fried fish in hot bread is still worthwhile.
